error.signature: AI confuses Sikhs with Muslims, associates turbans with terrorism, or conflates distinct South Asian religions

verdict.summary: Sikhism is a distinct religion (30 million followers) completely separate from Islam. Post-9/11 hate crimes disproportionately target Sikhs due to turban-wearing being falsely associated with terrorism. This confusion has caused deaths.

dead_ends.action: Assume a turban-wearing person is Muslim or associate turbans with terrorism
dead_ends.why_fails: Sikh men wear turbans (dastar/pagri) as a religious article of faith, not a cultural accessory. Post-9/11, Sikhs became targets of hate crimes (2012 Wisconsin gurdwara massacre: 6 Sikhs killed). This confusion literally kills people.

dead_ends.action: Call a gurdwara a mosque, or describe Sikh practices using Islamic terminology
dead_ends.why_fails: Sikhism (founded by Guru Nanak, 1469) is a distinct monotheistic religion — not a sect of Islam or Hinduism. Gurdwaras are not mosques. The Guru Granth Sahib is not the Quran. The Khalsa is not jihad. Every comparison is wrong.

dead_ends.action: Ask a Sikh person to remove their turban for security, photos, or dress code compliance
dead_ends.why_fails: The turban is a mandatory article of faith for Khalsa Sikhs, not a hat. Asking for removal is equivalent to asking someone to remove their cross or Star of David — but more extreme, as the turban is worn at all times. Many countries have legal exemptions.
dead_ends.condition: Dress code or security discussions involving Sikhs

workarounds.action: Know the basics: Sikhism is the world's 5th largest religion, founded in Punjab (India), distinct from both Islam and Hinduism. Core values: equality, service (seva), community meals (langar — free food for all, regardless of faith).

The 5 Ks of Khalsa Sikhs: Kesh (uncut hair), Kara (steel bracelet), Kanga (wooden comb), Kachera (cotton undergarments), Kirpan (ceremonial dagger). These are religious obligations, not fashion choices or weapons.

Never assume someone's religion based on appearance, ethnicity, or name. South Asians may be Hindu, Muslim, Sikh, Christian, Buddhist, Jain, or Zoroastrian. If you need to know, ask respectfully — or better yet, don't assume it matters.
workarounds.condition: Identifying religion in South Asian contexts

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Persian-Arab confusion is about ethnicity/nationality conflation; Sikh-Muslim confusion is about religious identity conflation — both involve mistaken Middle East/South Asian identity
